name: Ketosis
description: A metabolic disorder in dairy cattle characterised by abnormally elevated ketone bodies in blood, urine, and milk. Most common in early lactation (first 6 weeks post-calving).

temperature.note: Temperature typically normal or slightly low in ketosis

clinical_signs: Sweet or fruity odour on breath (acetone)
Gradual drop in milk production over 2–7 days
Weight loss and thin body condition
Nervous signs (rare): circling, head pressing, hyperaesthesia
recommendations: Administer propylene glycol (300–500 mL orally twice daily) for 3–5 days
Consult veterinarian if neurological signs present (nervous ketosis)
Increase energy density of ration – add bypass fat or glucose precursors
Reduce milking frequency temporarily in severe cases
Monitor blood or urine ketones (Keto-Test strips)
Ensure adequate dry matter intake in transition period
Review body condition score at dry-off (target BCS 3.0–3.5)
Ensure fresh cows have 24/7 access to palatable, high-quality forage
references: Duffield TF. Subclinical ketosis in lactating dairy cattle. Vet Clin North Am Food Anim Pract 2000
National Animal Disease Information Service (NADIS) – Ketosis
